title: Chat System - System Design Interview
tagline: Design a WebSocket-based chat platform for one-to-one and small group messaging across devices.
problem: Build a chat system for web and mobile clients that supports low-latency one-to-one messages, small group chat, online presence, push notifications, and multi-device synchronization for about 50 million daily active users.

functional: Send and receive one-to-one text messages with low latency.
Support small group conversations up to about 100 members.
Show online presence for friends or conversation participants.
Deliver messages to multiple devices for the same user.
Send push notifications when the recipient is offline.
nonFunctional: Horizontal scaling of stateless APIs and stateful WebSocket servers.
Graceful reconnection and replay after mobile network interruptions.
requirements.designPrompt: Keep the split between stateless control-plane services and stateful connection servers clear. The strongest answer shows how messages move from sender to durable storage to recipient devices.

capacity.label: Connection scale
capacity.value: Millions of concurrent WebSocket connections at peak.

capacity.label: Group size
capacity.value: <= 100 members, which supports per-recipient inbox fanout.

capacity.label: Message ID shape
capacity.value: Time-sortable and unique inside a conversation.

capacity.label: Presence heartbeat
capacity.value: Seconds to tens of seconds depending on mobile battery policy.

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: 50 million daily active users across mobile and web. Text messages are less than 100 KB. Groups are small, so fanout-on-write is viable for group delivery. Message history is retained indefinitely in a distributed key-value store.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: Capacity pressure comes from long-lived connections and fanout, not from complex queries. Estimate connection memory, message throughput, and inbox storage separately.

dataModel.name: user_profile
dataModel.note: Basic user data used by chat control-plane APIs.

dataModel.name: message
dataModel.note: Immutable message record stored by conversation and message ID.

steps.title: 1. Choose the Client Protocol
steps.description: The system needs bidirectional low-latency messaging. WebSocket is the right default because both client and server can push events over one long-lived connection.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should the client poll for messages, use long polling, or maintain a WebSocket connection?

concepts.definition: The client repeatedly asks the server whether new messages exist.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is simple but wasteful and adds latency.
concepts.group: Choose the Client Protocol

concepts.definition: The server holds a request open until data is available or a timeout fires.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves latency but still creates repeated request churn.
concepts.group: Choose the Client Protocol

concepts.definition: A persistent full-duplex connection between client and server.
concepts.whyItMatters: It fits chat because both sides need to send events immediately.
concepts.group: Choose the Client Protocol

options.title: WebSocket
options.description: Use persistent bidirectional connections for active chat sessions.

options.title: Long polling fallback
options.description: Use long polling where WebSockets are blocked by proxies or older clients.
tradeoffs: More overhead and worse latency than WebSocket.

flows.name: Connection setup
flows.note: A client authenticates, discovers a nearby chat server, then opens a WebSocket.

deepDives: Keep REST for control-plane actions like group management, but use WebSocket for message delivery.
bottlenecks: Connection memory and file descriptors on chat servers.
Mobile reconnection churn.
talkingPoints: Say what happens when WebSockets are unavailable: a fallback can exist without being the primary architecture.

steps.title: 2. Separate Stateless APIs From Service Discovery
steps.description: Authentication, profiles, group management, and discovery can scale as stateless services. Service discovery chooses a chat server based on geography, load, and capacity.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which parts of chat should be stateless control-plane services?

concepts.definition: Services that manage metadata, configuration, and routing decisions.
concepts.whyItMatters: These are easier to scale statelessly than active chat connections.
concepts.group: Separate Stateless APIs From Service Discovery

concepts.definition: A service that returns a suitable server endpoint for a client.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents clients from randomly landing on overloaded or distant chat servers.
concepts.group: Separate Stateless APIs From Service Discovery

concepts.definition: A client remains attached to one chat server while the WebSocket is alive.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets the server hold connection state efficiently.
concepts.group: Separate Stateless APIs From Service Discovery

deepDives: Discovery can be implemented with systems like ZooKeeper, etcd, or a custom placement service; the interview focus is the routing decision.
bottlenecks: Stale discovery data can send clients to bad servers.
Authentication token checks must be fast during reconnect storms.
talkingPoints: Draw this before message delivery so the stateful/stateless boundary is explicit.

steps.title: 3. Run a Stateful Chat Connection Fleet
steps.description: Chat servers hold active WebSocket connections and route events to connected devices. A connection registry records which server currently owns each online device.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we route a message to the right server when recipients may be connected elsewhere?

concepts.definition: A fast mapping from user/device to active chat server.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets the sender-side server find recipient connections.
concepts.group: Run a Stateful Chat Connection Fleet

concepts.definition: A service that keeps live per-connection state in memory.
concepts.whyItMatters: It needs careful draining and reconnect handling during deploys.
concepts.group: Run a Stateful Chat Connection Fleet

concepts.definition: Signals that slow senders when a server or recipient cannot keep up.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents unbounded queues from exhausting memory.
concepts.group: Run a Stateful Chat Connection Fleet

flows.name: Online delivery routing
flows.note: The sender server stores the message and then uses the registry to find recipient devices.

deepDives: The registry can be Redis-like because entries are ephemeral and refreshed by heartbeats. Durable messages live elsewhere.
bottlenecks: Cross-server fanout latency.
Registry hot keys for very active users.
talkingPoints: Avoid putting permanent chat history in the connection registry.

steps.title: 4. Store History in a Distributed Message Store
steps.description: Messages are immutable and read mostly by conversation order. A key-value or wide-column store keyed by conversation ID and sortable message ID fits history reads better than a generic relational table at this scale.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should message history be keyed so scrollback and sync are efficient?

concepts.definition: Messages for a conversation are clustered under the same partition key.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes recent history and incremental sync efficient.
concepts.group: Store History in a Distributed Message Store

concepts.definition: An identifier that preserves creation order without relying on database auto-increment globally.
concepts.whyItMatters: It supports pagination and per-device cursors.
concepts.group: Store History in a Distributed Message Store

concepts.definition: A message record is appended rather than updated in place.
concepts.whyItMatters: It simplifies replication, fanout, and audit history.
concepts.group: Store History in a Distributed Message Store

flows.name: Message persistence
flows.note: Message IDs are assigned before fanout so every recipient sees the same ordering within a conversation.

deepDives: Global ordering across all conversations is unnecessary; per-conversation ordering is the product requirement.
bottlenecks: Very large groups or celebrity conversations can create hot partitions.
Clock-dependent IDs need skew handling.
talkingPoints: Explain why relational storage is still fine for users, profiles, and group metadata.

options.name: Wide-column store keyed by conversation + message id (default)
pros: Messages are immutable and read mostly by conversation order, which maps directly to (conversation_id, message_id) keys.
Scrollback and reconnect-sync become a single contiguous range scan within a partition.
Scales horizontally to chat volumes far beyond a single relational table.
cons: Sortable message ids must be generated (snowflake-style) to keep order without a global counter.
Cross-conversation queries (rare here) are awkward in this key layout.
Less ad-hoc query flexibility than SQL.

options.name: Relational table with composite index
pros: Familiar SQL with transactions and a (conversation_id, created_at) index for ordered reads.
Flexible queries for moderation, reporting, and edits.
Strong consistency on a single primary.
cons: A single huge messages table needs manual sharding to handle chat-scale write volume.
Index maintenance on a write-heavy append workload becomes a bottleneck.
Range reads compete with writes on the same hot rows.

options.name: Append-only per-conversation log
pros: Each conversation is a partitioned log where offset is the order, so no separate id generator is needed.
Append throughput is high and reads are sequential offset scans.
Other consumers (sync queues, search indexers) read the same ordered log.
cons: Random access by message id is weaker than a keyed store.
Operating a partitioned log (retention, compaction) is heavier infrastructure.
A partition leader failover briefly stalls that conversation's writes.

steps.title: 5. Deliver One-to-One Messages to Online and Offline Users
steps.description: After persistence, the system enqueues delivery work. Online devices receive WebSocket events; offline users get push notifications and catch up from durable history later.
steps.decisionPrompt: When should the system acknowledge a send: after persistence, after fanout, or after recipient delivery?

concepts.definition: A queue or inbox of messages waiting for a user or device.
concepts.whyItMatters: It decouples sender latency from recipient connectivity.
concepts.group: Deliver One-to-One Messages to Online and Offline Users

concepts.definition: A platform notification sent through APNs, FCM, or a similar provider.
concepts.whyItMatters: It wakes offline mobile users without keeping the app active.
concepts.group: Deliver One-to-One Messages to Online and Offline Users

concepts.definition: The same client message ID can be retried without creating duplicates.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is required for unreliable mobile networks.
concepts.group: Deliver One-to-One Messages to Online and Offline Users

flows.name: Offline recipient
flows.note: The message is durable before push; the push notification is only a wake-up signal.

deepDives: A common interview mistake is treating push notification as delivery. Real delivery comes from sync against stored messages.
bottlenecks: Push providers are external dependencies.
Per-user queues need retention and trimming policies.
talkingPoints: Define sent, delivered, and read states separately.

options.name: Ack after durable persistence (default)
pros: The sender is acked once the message is durably stored, so it survives crashes and the recipient can always catch up from history.
Delivery work (online push or offline notification) is enqueued after persistence, decoupling send from delivery.
Clear semantics: an acked message is never lost.
cons: An ack does not yet mean the recipient received it — delivery is still pending.
Showing 'delivered'/'read' needs separate downstream signals.
Persistence latency is on the send path.

options.name: Ack after fanout/enqueue only
pros: Lowest send latency — ack as soon as the message is queued for delivery.
Decouples the sender entirely from storage and delivery timing.
High send throughput under bursts.
cons: If the queue or worker loses the message before persistence, it can be lost despite the ack.
Recipient catch-up from durable history is not guaranteed at ack time.
Weaker durability guarantee to reason about.

options.name: Ack after recipient delivery
pros: The sender's ack means the recipient actually received the message — strongest delivery semantic.
Naturally drives accurate 'delivered' receipts.
No ambiguity about whether the other side has it.
cons: Send blocks until the recipient is reachable, which fails outright when they are offline.
Couples the sender's latency to the recipient's connectivity.
Doesn't fit a system where most recipients are intermittently offline.

steps.title: 6. Use Fanout-on-Write for Small Groups
steps.description: With groups capped around 100 members, writing each message to member inboxes is practical and makes reads simple and fast.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should group messages be copied to each recipient inbox or fanned out when users read?

concepts.definition: The system creates delivery work for each recipient when the message is sent.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes recipient reads fast for small groups.
concepts.group: Use Fanout-on-Write for Small Groups

concepts.definition: The system computes visible messages when a user opens the conversation.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids write amplification but makes reads more expensive.
concepts.group: Use Fanout-on-Write for Small Groups

concepts.definition: The member list used at send time.
concepts.whyItMatters: It defines who should receive the message even if the group changes later.
concepts.group: Use Fanout-on-Write for Small Groups

deepDives: The group-size limit is the reason fanout-on-write is acceptable. Large public channels need a different architecture.
bottlenecks: A 100-member group multiplies write work by about 100.
Membership lookups need caching.
talkingPoints: Tie the design choice directly to the explicit group-size requirement.

options.name: Fanout-on-write to member inboxes (default)
pros: With groups capped around 100 members, copying each message to member inboxes is cheap.
Reads are trivial and fast: a member just reads their own inbox.
Online push and offline catch-up both work off the same per-member inbox.
cons: Write amplification grows with group size — wrong for very large groups.
Storage duplicates each message across all member inboxes.
A burst to a big group multiplies write load.

options.name: Fanout-on-read from a shared group timeline
pros: One copy per message in a shared group store — no write amplification.
Scales to very large groups where copying to every inbox would explode.
Cheap, simple writes regardless of member count.
cons: Every read fans out to assemble the member's view, adding read-time cost.
Unread counts and per-member state need extra bookkeeping.
Slower reads for members in many active groups.

steps.title: 7. Synchronize Multiple Devices With Per-Device Cursors
steps.description: Each device tracks the highest message it has received per conversation. On reconnect, it fetches messages after that cursor and acknowledges progress.
steps.decisionPrompt: How can a phone, tablet, and browser all catch up without missing or duplicating messages?

concepts.definition: A per-device marker of the last message seen or delivered.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes reconnect and multi-device catch-up deterministic.
concepts.group: Synchronize Multiple Devices With Per-Device Cursors

concepts.definition: A message can be delivered more than once during retries.
concepts.whyItMatters: The client must deduplicate by stable message ID.
concepts.group: Synchronize Multiple Devices With Per-Device Cursors

concepts.definition: A user-visible state derived from client acknowledgements.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is separate from durable storage and delivery queue acceptance.
concepts.group: Synchronize Multiple Devices With Per-Device Cursors

flows.name: Reconnect sync
flows.note: The device sends its cursor, receives the gap, then advances the cursor after processing.

deepDives: Use device-level cursors for delivery state and user-level markers for read state if the product distinguishes them.
bottlenecks: Large offline gaps can create expensive catch-up reads.
Clients must handle duplicates gracefully.
talkingPoints: Mention that the message store remains the source of truth.

steps.title: 8. Add Presence With Heartbeats and Pub/Sub
steps.description: Presence servers record online state from active connections. Heartbeats refresh liveness, while pub/sub pushes presence changes to interested friends or conversations.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should the system avoid showing users as offline during short mobile network blips?

concepts.definition: A periodic signal from client or connection server that indicates liveness.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps presence state fresh without requiring every action to update status.
concepts.group: Add Presence With Heartbeats and Pub/Sub

concepts.definition: A delay before marking a user offline after disconnect.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces visible flapping on unstable networks.
concepts.group: Add Presence With Heartbeats and Pub/Sub

concepts.definition: Publishing state changes to subscribers.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids every client polling for friend status.
concepts.group: Add Presence With Heartbeats and Pub/Sub

deepDives: Presence is eventually consistent. It should be useful, not a source of hard correctness guarantees.
bottlenecks: Presence fanout can be much larger than message fanout.
Heartbeat intervals trade freshness against battery and network cost.
talkingPoints: Use a grace period to handle quick reconnects.

options.name: Heartbeats + pub/sub push (default)
pros: Presence is derived from connection heartbeats, so a short mobile blip doesn't immediately flip a user offline.
Changes are pushed to interested friends/conversations via pub/sub, keeping status fresh.
Best-effort and ephemeral, so it stays off the durable message path.
cons: Pub/sub fanout of frequent changes can be heavy for users with many watchers.
Tuning heartbeat timeout trades responsiveness against false-offline flapping.
State must be re-derived from live connections after a presence-server restart.

options.name: Client polling for friends' presence
pros: Friends' status is fetched on a timer — no pub/sub fanout to maintain.
Simple server side: answer a batch status query from the presence store.
Avoids pushing every transient change to every watcher.
cons: Polling interval trades freshness against request volume across all clients.
Status feels laggy compared to pushed updates.
Wastes requests when nothing has changed.

steps.title: 9. Close With Recovery and Operability
steps.description: A production chat system needs draining for stateful servers, retry and deduplication for mobile clients, metrics for delivery lag, and replay tools for stuck queues.
steps.decisionPrompt: What makes the chat system reliable during deploys, server failures, and mobile reconnect storms?

concepts.definition: Stop accepting new connections and let existing connections migrate before shutting down a server.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces message disruption during deploys.
concepts.group: Close With Recovery and Operability

concepts.definition: A queue for delivery work that repeatedly fails.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gives operators a way to inspect and replay bad messages.
concepts.group: Close With Recovery and Operability

concepts.definition: The time between message acceptance and recipient availability.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the core operational signal for chat health.
concepts.group: Close With Recovery and Operability

deepDives: The design is not complete until you can deploy chat servers without dropping all active clients.
bottlenecks: Reconnect storms can overload auth and discovery.
Bad queue consumers can create growing delivery lag.
talkingPoints: Name concrete metrics: connected clients, send latency, durable append latency, delivery queue depth, push error rate.

functional.how: WebSockets keep active clients connected, the connection registry routes online delivery, and durable message append happens before fanout.
Groups capped around 100 members use membership lookup plus fanout-on-write into member sync queues.
Messages are immutable records in the message store, while device cursors let every device replay missing messages independently.
Heartbeats update presence state and pub/sub distributes status changes without polling every friend list.
Client idempotency, cursor replay, dead-letter queues, metrics, and graceful server draining recover from reconnects and deploys.

nonFunctional.how: Stateless APIs scale separately from stateful chat servers, and service discovery spreads connections by location and capacity.
At-least-once delivery, dedupe by message ID, push wakeups, and reconnect sync handle dropped mobile connections.
Delivery lag, queue depth, push errors, connection count, and replay tooling are part of the final operations layer.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design splits chat into stateless control-plane APIs and stateful real-time delivery. Clients discover a nearby connection gateway, hold WebSocket sessions on chat servers, messages are durably stored before delivery, per-user sync queues and device cursors support offline and multi-device replay, push notifications wake offline clients, and presence is handled by a separate heartbeat and pub/sub path.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk through sending one message from a phone to a recipient who has one online device and one offline device.

concepts.definition: Profiles, groups, auth, and discovery are separate from the live message path.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps long-lived WebSocket state from complicating ordinary metadata APIs.

concepts.definition: The system appends the message before fanning it out.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets offline clients recover from history instead of trusting volatile pushes.

concepts.definition: Each device has its own sync progress.
concepts.whyItMatters: It solves multi-device replay without forcing all devices to be online together.

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.

options.title: Large-channel variant
options.description: For very large rooms, store once per channel and fan out on read. This is less suitable for the prompt because groups are capped at about 100 members.

flows.name: Online and offline message delivery
flows.note: A message is stored once, queued for recipients, delivered over WebSocket when possible, and pushed as a wake-up signal otherwise.

messages.label: one device on Chat Server B

flows.name: Reconnect sync
flows.note: A device never depends on push history; it uses a cursor to replay missed messages from durable storage.

deepDives: The message store is the source of truth. WebSocket delivery, sync queues, push notifications, and presence are delivery mechanisms around it.
bottlenecks: Connection registry hot spots for highly active users.
Reconnect storms after mobile or regional outages.
Presence fanout can exceed message fanout.
talkingPoints: Use fanout-on-write because group size is bounded.
Differentiate sent, delivered, and read receipts.
Mention graceful draining for stateful chat servers.

followUps: How would the design change for public channels with millions of subscribers?
How would you add end-to-end encryption?
What ordering guarantees do you provide inside and across conversations?
How do you prevent duplicate sends during client retries?

links.groupDescription: Production references for realtime messaging, collaboration, media storage, and streaming delivery.
links.title: How Discord Stores Trillions of Messages
links.why: Production message-storage migration with Cassandra, ScyllaDB, partitions, and hot data concerns.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Cassandra: A Decentralized Structured Storage System
links.why: Useful for high-write distributed storage, eventual consistency, and wide-column message histories.

links.groupDescription: Production references for realtime messaging, collaboration, media storage, and streaming delivery.
links.title: The WebSocket Protocol
links.why: Core protocol reference for persistent bidirectional browser connections.

links.groupDescription: Messaging, event streaming, delivery semantics, and async processing references.
links.title: Apache Kafka Design
links.why: Explains Kafka's log, partitions, consumer groups, replication, and operational model.

links.groupDescription: Messaging, event streaming, delivery semantics, and async processing references.
links.title: Redis Pub/Sub
links.why: Useful for lightweight realtime fanout and its limitations compared with durable streams.

links.groupDescription: Production references for realtime messaging, collaboration, media storage, and streaming delivery.
links.title: Firebase Realtime Database
links.why: Concrete hosted realtime sync model for presence, fanout, and client subscriptions.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Monitoring Distributed Systems
links.why: Grounds monitoring and alerting decisions in user-visible reliability signals.

api.description: Open a persistent chat connection after authentication.
Send a message over the active WebSocket.
Acknowledge delivery or read progress from a device.
Fetch history after a cursor for sync or scrollback.

description: Design a WebSocket-based chat platform for one-to-one and small group messaging across devices.
